title: Call Stored Procedure Components
text: Call Stored Procedure Components To call a stored procedure component, use the ProcScript activate statement. Use the activate statement to invoke the stored procedure as follows: activate InstanceName . OperationName ( Parameter1 ,... ParameterN ) InstanceName is the name of the component instance. This has a maximum length of 16 bytes for Uniface components, or 32 bytes for non-Uniface components. OperationName is the required operation of the stored procedure component. This is mapped to a stored procedure name. Therefore, the operation name must resemble the stored procedure name. Parameter1 through ParameterN are the operation’s parameters. The number and order of the parameters you specify must match those within the stored procedure. The operation parameter name is mapped to a formal parameter name within the stored procedure. Therefore, all operation parameter names must resemble the stored procedure parameter names. For information on DBMS-specific requirements or restrictions, see the relevant connector information. The service stored procedure, ENDOFMONTH, has two basic parameters. The first is an input string parameter specifying the month, and the second is an output numeric parameter specifying a derived monthly total. The ENDOFMONTH component is located on a remote machine, MyHOST. The client assignment file contains the following: $STORED_PROCS tcp:MyHost|User|Password| + SRU:||| ENDOFMONTH = $STORED_PROCS:ENDOFMONTH Copy The stored procedure is activated as follows: activate "ENDOFMONTH".ENDOFMONTH ($in$, $out$) Copy Related Links activate Calls an operation on a component instance. Naming Specifications for Service Store Procedures For Service Stored Procedures, the signature of the Stored Procedure Component maps the names of Uniface components, operations, and parameters to the name of a package, procedure, and parameter names in the procedure. SQL and Stored Procedures Support For SQL-enabled databases, you can use the sql ProcScript instruction and SQL Editor to send SQL instructions to the database. Some databases also support stored procedures. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
